meta.title: Psychiatry Practice Software | Behavioral RTM & No-Show Reduction | clinIQ
meta.description: Complete guide to psychiatry practice operations. $9,000/month in behavioral RTM. 25% no-show reduction. Private check-in. Session time visibility. Prior auth for medications.

hero.subhead: Your patients already report symptoms between visits — that's billable behavioral RTM. Your no-show rate already costs you $500/day. Your check-in process already needs to protect privacy. clinIQ captures the revenue, reduces no-shows, and treats patients with dignity.

sections.content:
Psychiatry practices face operational challenges that other specialties don't understand.

No-show rates: Psychiatric patients have higher no-show rates than general medical patients — often 15-25%. A practice seeing 20 patients/day at 20% no-show loses 4 appointments daily. At $200/visit average, that's $800/day, $200,000/year in lost revenue.

Privacy sensitivity: Patients don't want their psychiatric care broadcast in a waiting room. Traditional check-in — stating your name and appointment type at a reception desk — violates that privacy.

Session timing: Psychiatric visits vary from 15-minute med checks to 60-minute therapy sessions. The schedule template rarely matches reality. Providers run behind because 30-minute slots take 45 minutes.

Between-visit monitoring: Psychiatric conditions fluctuate between visits. Mood, sleep, anxiety, medication side effects — patients should be tracking this. Most don't systematically. This data is clinically valuable and now billable as RTM.

Medication authorization: Prior auths for psychiatric medications (especially newer/branded drugs) are common and time-consuming. Staff spends hours on hold with payers.

This guide covers systems that address each challenge.

CMS finalized behavioral health RTM codes effective 2024. Psychiatric conditions — depression, anxiety, bipolar disorder, PTSD, and others — now qualify for Remote Therapeutic Monitoring billing.

Your patients should be tracking symptoms between visits anyway. Mood logs. Sleep quality. Anxiety levels. Medication compliance and side effects. PHQ-9 and GAD-7 scores. This data informs treatment decisions.

RTM systematizes that tracking and makes it billable.

**What psychiatric patients track:**

Mood: Daily or weekly mood ratings. Identifying patterns, triggers, cycles.

Sleep: Hours, quality, disturbances. Sleep problems correlate with psychiatric symptoms.

Anxiety: Anxiety levels, panic episodes, avoidance behaviors.

Medication: Compliance, missed doses, side effects, perceived effectiveness.

Functional status: Work attendance, social engagement, self-care, crisis events.

**The CPT codes:**

98975: RTM setup — one-time enrollment. ~$20-25.
98976: RTM device supply (behavioral health). ~$50-60/month.
98980: RTM treatment management — first 20 minutes clinical review. ~$50-70/month.
98981: Additional 20-minute increments.

Typical monthly billing: $100-140/patient.

**The math for a psychiatry practice:**

75 appropriate patients × $120/month = $9,000/month = $108,000/year.

This is revenue for clinical work that's already supposed to happen — monitoring symptoms between visits and adjusting treatment accordingly.

**What clinIQ does for behavioral RTM:**

Patient app: Mood tracking, sleep logging, medication compliance, symptom check-ins. HIPAA-compliant, private, stigma-free.

Clinical dashboards: Which patients are stable? Which are declining? Which need urgent outreach?

Alert thresholds: PHQ-9 score spiking? Missed medications? Sleep deteriorating? Alerts trigger proactive intervention.

Automatic time tracking: Every data review documented toward billing threshold.

**The clinical value:**

Beyond billing, RTM improves care. You see patterns between visits. You catch deterioration early. You have objective data instead of patient recall. Better outcomes and better revenue.

Psychiatric patients have higher no-show rates than general medical patients. Contributing factors:

- Ambivalence about treatment (especially early in care)
- Symptoms themselves (depression reduces motivation, anxiety increases avoidance)
- Stigma about attending psychiatric appointments
- Transportation and life instability
- Long waits between scheduling and appointment

No-show rates of 15-25% are common in psychiatry. A practice with 20 scheduled appointments/day at 20% no-show sees only 16 patients. Four slots wasted daily.

**The revenue impact:**

4 no-shows/day × $200/visit = $800/day lost = $200,000/year.

Reducing no-shows from 20% to 15% recovers 1 patient/day = $50,000/year.

**No-show reduction strategies:**

1. Confirmation sequence: Day-before and morning-of confirmations. Text preferred — psychiatric patients often don't answer phone calls.

2. Ease of rescheduling: If patient can't make it, make rescheduling frictionless. A rescheduled appointment is better than a no-show.

3. Short wait times to appointment: Patients scheduled 6 weeks out have higher no-show rates than those scheduled 2 weeks out.

4. RTM engagement: Patients engaged in between-visit tracking are more invested in treatment. They show up.

5. Private, stigma-reduced experience: Patients who feel respected are more likely to return.

**What clinIQ does for no-shows:**

Confirmation automation: Text confirmations at configurable intervals. Easy reply to confirm or request reschedule.

No-show prediction: Patients with history of no-shows flagged for extra confirmation or overbooking consideration.

Quick-fill for cancellations: When patients cancel, waitlist patients notified automatically.

No-show tracking: Rates by provider, by day, by appointment type. Data identifies patterns for intervention.

Traditional medical check-in doesn't work for psychiatry.

Patient approaches reception desk. Receptionist asks (audibly): 'Name?' 'Date of birth?' 'You're here to see Dr. Smith for your psychiatry appointment?'

Everyone in the waiting room heard. Patient cringes. Trust damaged.

**Why privacy matters more in psychiatry:**

Stigma: Mental health stigma persists. Patients don't want others knowing they're receiving psychiatric care.

Safety: Some patients (domestic violence, stalking situations) need their whereabouts private.

Therapeutic relationship: The clinical relationship starts at the front door. A privacy violation at check-in undermines trust before the session begins.

**What clinIQ does for private check-in:**

Kiosk check-in: Patient checks in on tablet. No verbal exchange. No audible confirmation of appointment type.

Phone check-in: Patient checks in from their phone before arriving. Walks in already checked in.

Minimal identifiers: Check-in screens don't display 'PSYCHIATRY' prominently. The experience is medical care, not stigma.

Car waiting option: Patient can wait in car and receive text when provider is ready. Zero waiting room time.

**The mixed waiting room problem:**

Some psychiatry practices share space with other specialties. A patient seeing a psychiatrist waits next to a patient seeing a cardiologist. The psychiatry patient feels exposed.

clinIQ's car-waiting option eliminates this. Patient arrives, checks in from phone, waits in car, gets texted when ready. No waiting room exposure at all.

Psychiatric visits vary in duration more than most specialties:

- Med check: 15-20 minutes
- Therapy session: 45-60 minutes
- Initial evaluation: 60-90 minutes
- Crisis visit: Variable

The schedule template often doesn't match reality. A '30-minute follow-up' actually takes 45 minutes because the patient is in crisis. The provider runs behind. The next patient waits. Nobody knows how long.

**The visibility problem:**

Provider is with a patient. How long have they been in session? Is this running long? Should the next patient be warned about a delay?

Nobody knows. The front desk doesn't want to interrupt. The next patient sits wondering.

**What clinIQ does for session visibility:**

Session timer: When patient is roomed, timer starts. Current session duration visible (to staff, not patients).

Running-long alerts: Session exceeds scheduled duration by X minutes — alert to staff. They can inform waiting patients.

Provider status: Provider in session, provider between patients, provider documenting. Visible without interrupting.

Schedule accuracy data: Which appointment types consistently run long? Data to adjust schedule templates.

**The documentation buffer:**

Psychiatry requires significant documentation. Providers need time between patients to chart.

clinIQ can track 'documentation time' separately from session time. Provider finishes session → documentation time starts → provider marks ready for next patient.

This makes documentation time visible and protectable, rather than something that bleeds into the next appointment.

Psychiatric medications frequently require prior authorization, especially:

- Newer/branded medications when generics exist
- ADHD medications (stimulants)
- Antipsychotics (especially newer ones)
- Specialty medications for treatment-resistant conditions

Each auth requires: contacting the payer, completing forms, providing clinical justification, waiting for approval, handling denials. This takes staff hours per week.

**What clinIQ does for medication auth:**

Auth status tracking: Pending, approved, denied — per patient per medication. No wondering 'did that Vyvanse auth come through?'

Expiration alerts: Auth expires in 30 days. Patient will need refill. Renewal needed.

Clinical documentation support: Diagnosis, prior medication trials, clinical justification — accessible for auth submission.

**The step therapy problem:**

Payers often require step therapy — patient must fail generic before branded is approved. Documentation of prior failures is critical.

clinIQ tracks medication history:
- Patient tried sertraline × 8 weeks, inadequate response
- Patient tried escitalopram × 6 weeks, side effects
- Patient tried venlafaxine × 8 weeks, partial response

This history, documented systematically, supports authorization for next-line agents.

Psychiatry implementation focuses on RTM enrollment, no-show reduction, and private check-in.

**Week 1: Configuration**

We configure for your practice: appointment types, session durations, check-in method, RTM templates.

Behavioral RTM templates configured: mood tracking, sleep, anxiety, medication compliance, PHQ-9/GAD-7.

**Week 2: Training and Go-Live**

Front desk learns private check-in. Providers learn RTM dashboard. Staff learns confirmation workflows.

Go-live with support.

**Week 3 and Beyond**

RTM enrollment beginning. No-show reduction strategies active. Private check-in running.

**ROI Timeline:**

Month 1: Private check-in immediate. RTM enrollment starting. Confirmation automation active.

Month 3: RTM billing at scale. No-show reduction measurable. Session visibility improving schedule accuracy.

Month 6: Full RTM revenue. Sustained no-show improvement. Medication auth tracking running.

**ROI Math:**

RTM revenue: 75 patients × $120/month = $9,000/month = $108,000/year

No-show reduction: 5% improvement × 20 patients/day × $200/visit × 250 days = $50,000/year

Combined: $150,000+/year additional revenue.

**Investment:**

Professional: $499/month — Full suite including RTM, check-in, scheduling, auth tracking

Implementation: $750 one-time — Configuration, training, go-live support

faqs.q: Is behavioral health RTM now billable?
faqs.a: Yes. CMS finalized behavioral health RTM codes effective 2024. Depression, anxiety, bipolar, PTSD, and other psychiatric conditions qualify. CPT 98976 for device supply, 98980/98981 for treatment management.
Kiosk or phone-based check-in. No verbal exchange at reception. No audible confirmation of appointment type. Option to wait in car and receive text when provider is ready.
Yes. Automated confirmations, easy rescheduling, waitlist fill, and RTM engagement all reduce no-shows. 20-25% improvement is achievable for practices with high baseline no-show rates.

faqs.q: How do you track session time?
faqs.a: Timer starts when patient is roomed. Running-long alerts when session exceeds scheduled duration. Documentation time tracked separately. Data identifies which appointment types need schedule adjustment.
Auth status tracked per patient per medication. Expiration alerts. Prior medication trial history documented to support step therapy appeals.

faqs.q: Does RTM work for therapy-only patients?
faqs.a: Yes. Therapy patients can track mood, sleep, and therapy homework completion between sessions. The data informs treatment and qualifies for RTM billing.

faqs.q: What if patients don't want to track?
faqs.a: RTM is offered, not required. Most psychiatric patients appreciate the structure and engagement. Enrollment rates of 60-70% are typical for appropriate patients.

faqs.q: How do you handle crisis situations flagged by RTM?
faqs.a: Alert thresholds can be configured for safety concerns. High PHQ-9 scores or specific responses trigger immediate alerts. Crisis protocols can be built into the workflow.

testimonial.quote: Our patients were mortified by traditional check-in. Now they check in from their phones and wait in their cars. Satisfaction scores went up, and we're billing $8K/month in RTM.
